perturbation.original_text: FASC shall create and maintain all necessary books and records in accordance with all applicable laws, rules and regulations, including but not limited to records required by Section 31(a) of the 1940 Act and the rules thereunder, as the same may be amended from time to time, pertaining to the Services performed by it and not otherwise created and maintained by another party. Where applicable, such records shall be maintained by FASC for the periods and in the places required by Rule 31a-2 under the 1940 Act. The books and records pertaining to any Account which are in the possession of FAS shall be the property of such Account. The Account, or its owners or authorized representatives, shall have access to such books and records at all times during FASC's normal business hours. Upon reasonable request, copies of any such books and records shall be provided promptly by FASC to the Account or the Account's owners or authorized representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: FASC shall create and maintain books and records pertaining to the Services performed by it. The books and records pertaining to any Account which are in the possession of FAS shall be the property of such Account. The Account, or its owners or authorized representatives, shall have access to such books and records at all times during FASC's normal business hours.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the explicit requirement for FASC to maintain records in accordance with applicable laws, rules, and regulations, including Section 31(a) of the 1940 Act and Rule 31a-2, thus weakening the compliance obligations of FASC and potentially violating the Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text removes the explicit requirement to adhere to Section 31(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940, which mandates registered investment companies and related parties to maintain specific records. The omission creates a contradiction by failing to ensure compliance with these legally mandated record-keeping requirements.

Accounts and records (a) Maintenance of records (1) In general Each registered investment company, and each underwriter, broker, dealer, or investment adviser that is a majority-owned subsidiary of such a company, shall maintain and preserve such records (as defined in section 78c(a)(37) of this title ) for such period or periods as the Commission, by rules and regulations, may prescribe as necessary or
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit requirement for FASC to maintain records in accordance with applicable laws, rules, and regulations, including Section 31(a) of the 1940 Act and Rule 31a-2. This creates a legal contradiction because Section 31(a), as indicated by the provided snippet '15 USC 80a-30: Accounts and records', mandates that registered investment companies and related parties 'shall maintain and preserve such records...for such period or periods as the Commission, by rules and regulations, may prescribe as necessary'. The omission in the modified text directly undermines this legal obligation, making it likely to fail legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: The Adviser shall indemnify FASC and shall hold FASC harmless from and against any liability to any Account or to any other person which may incurred by or asserted against FASC for any action taken or omitted by it in performing the Services in accordance with the above standards, and any expenses (including the reasonable fees and expenses of its counsel) which may be incurred by FASC in investigating or defending itself against the assertion of any such liability. FASC shall give prompt notice to the Adviser of the assertion of any claim or liability which is reasonably likely to result in a claim for indemnification under this Section; provided that the failure to give such notice, or any delay in giving such notice, shall not lessen the obligation of the Adviser to indemnify FASC except to the extent it results in actual prejudice.
perturbation.changed_text: The Adviser may indemnify FASC and may hold FASC harmless from liability asserted against FASC for action taken or omitted by it in performing the Services. FASC shall give notice to the Adviser of the assertion of any claim or liability.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall indemnify' to 'may indemnify', and removing 'in accordance with the above standards' the contract significantly reduces the indemnification obligation of the Adviser. The removal of 'which is reasonably likely to result in a claim for indemnification' removes a trigger for required notification. The original text provides that failure to provide notice would only lessen obligation to the extent that it resulted in actual prejudice, but removing this provision gives more leeway to the Adviser to not indemnify.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Investment Advisers Act of 1940, Section 206, prohibits investment advisers from engaging in fraudulent or deceptive practices. Weakening the indemnification clause may incentivize FASC to take undue risks, as they are less protected against liability, and decreasing the requirement for notification of claims or liabilities impacts the advisers ability to protect their clients' interests. This could be seen as an indirect violation of the duty to act in the best interest of clients, potentially violating the Act.

Prohibited transactions by investment advisers It shall be unlawful for any investment adviser by use of the mails or any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, directly or indirectly- (1) to employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud any client or prospective client; (2) to engage in any transaction, practice, or course of business which
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall indemnify' to 'may indemnify' weakens the indemnification obligation. According to the Investment Advisers Act of 1940, Section 206, advisers cannot engage in fraudulent or deceptive practices. By reducing the indemnification, the adviser may take undue risks because they are less protected against liability. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC 80b-6, states it is unlawful 'to employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud any client or prospective client; to engage in any transaction, practice, or course of business which' operates as a fraud or deceit. The modified text directly contradicts this law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be amended at any time by mutual written agreement of the parties hereto; provided, however, that no Amendment to this Agreement shall be effective with respect to any investment advisory services to be provided to any Account which is registered investment company unless, to the extent required by Section 15(a)(2) of the 1940 Act, such amendment has been approved both by the vote of a majority of the Board of the Account, including a majority of the members of the Board who are not parties to this Agreement or interested persons of any such party (other than as members of the Board), cast in person at a meeting called for that purpose and, where required by Section 15(a)(2) of the 1940 Act, on behalf of the Account by a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such Account as defined in Section 2(a)(42) of the 1940 Act.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be amended at any time by mutual written agreement of the parties hereto.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the explicit requirement for amendments affecting investment advisory services to registered investment companies to be approved by the board and, if required, by a majority of the outstanding voting securities as mandated by Section 15(a)(2) of the 1940 Act. Removing this safeguard weakens investor protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text omits the requirement for board and shareholder approval for amendments affecting investment advisory services, as outlined in Section 15(a)(2) of the Investment Company Act of 1940. This contradicts the Act by circumventing the investor protection mechanisms put in place to ensure that changes to advisory agreements are properly vetted and approved.

Contracts of advisers and underwriters (a) Written contract to serve or act as investment adviser; contents It shall be unlawful for any person to serve or act as investment adviser of a registered investment company, except pursuant to a written contract, which contract, whether with such registered company or with an investment adviser of such registered company, has
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly requires amendments affecting investment advisory services to registered investment companies to be approved by the board and, if required, by a majority of the outstanding voting securities, in accordance with Section 15(a)(2) of the 1940 Act. The changed text removes this explicit requirement, thus removing a legally mandated investor protection, creating a legal contradiction under the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to 15 USC 80a-15, which is the US Code version of Section 15 of the Investment Company Act of 1940, showing it to be valid law.
